questions.question: According to Gallup research on strengths-based approaches, what are the outcomes for people who use their strengths daily?

options.text: No measurable difference compared to those who don't use strengths
6 times more likely to be engaged at work and 3 times more likely to report excellent quality of life
Slightly more productive but no impact on wellbeing
Improved performance but increased stress from overuse of strengths

questions.explanation: Gallup research across millions of employees found that people using their strengths daily are 6x more likely to be engaged at work and 3x more likely to report excellent quality of life. Yet most development plans focus 80% on weaknesses and 20% on strengths — exactly backward from what evidence supports. Strengths-based coaching builds from what's already working rather than solely fixing deficits.

questions.question: What is the key principle of strengths-based goal-setting compared to traditional deficit-focused goal-setting?

options.text: Ignore all weaknesses and only focus on strengths (weaknesses don't matter)

Identify the outcome you want to achieve, then design a path to that outcome BY USING your strengths rather than forcing yourself to fix weaknesses — same goal, more energizing and sustainable approach
Set easier goals that only require your strengths (avoid challenge)
Traditional and strengths-based goal-setting produce the same results

questions.explanation: Strengths-based goal-setting asks: How can you achieve this outcome using your natural strengths? Example: If you want influence at work but hate public speaking, leverage strengths (writing, one-on-one connection) instead of forcing presentations. Same outcome (influence), strengths-aligned path (faster, more energizing, sustainable). This doesn't mean ignore all weaknesses — address them only when they're true barriers, not as the primary development strategy.

questions.question: A client's top VIA strength is 'perseverance' — they always finish what they start despite obstacles. What is a potential 'shadow side' or overuse of this strength that a coach should watch for?

options.text: There is no downside — strengths are always beneficial

Overused perseverance can become stubbornness, inability to pivot when a goal no longer serves them, or grinding to the point of burnout. Coach should explore: 'Is this goal still aligned with what matters, or are you persisting because you feel you should?'
High perseverance means they never need coaching support
Perseverance is actually a weakness, not a strength

questions.explanation: Every strength has a shadow side when overused. Perseverance (finishing what you start) becomes stubbornness or compulsive completion when applied reflexively. The person may keep pushing toward a goal that no longer aligns with their values, or grind past healthy limits into burnout. Coaches help clients use strengths strategically (when appropriate) rather than automatically (all the time).

questions.question: What are the three main approaches for identifying a client's strengths taught in this lesson?

options.text: Asking their friends, checking social media, and guessing based on personality
Validated assessments (VIA, CliftonStrengths), strengths-spotting in conversation (listening for energy, excellence, flow), and mining past successes for patterns
Only use VIA assessment — other methods are unreliable
Strengths cannot be identified — people should just work on weaknesses

questions.explanation: Three complementary approaches: (1) Validated assessments (VIA free at VIACharacter.org, CliftonStrengths paid) provide structured identification of top strengths. (2) Strengths-spotting in conversation: listen for what energizes them, what they do excellently/effortlessly, rapid learning, flow states. (3) Past successes mining: analyze 3-5 success stories to find patterns in which strengths appear repeatedly. Use all three for comprehensive understanding.

questions.question: A client says: 'I need to network more for career growth, but I hate big events — I feel drained and awkward.' Their top strengths are curiosity, love of learning, and kindness. What's the strengths-based coaching response?

options.text: 'Push through the discomfort. Networking events are necessary — you'll get used to them.'

'Let's reframe networking through your strengths. What if instead of big events, you reached out one-on-one for informational interviews? Your curiosity makes you genuinely interested in what people do, and your kindness creates real connection. That's networking through strengths.'
'If events don't work for you, give up on networking — it's not that important.'
'Try to develop the strength of extraversion so you can enjoy events more.'

questions.explanation: Perfect strengths-based reframe: Same outcome (professional network), different path (one-on-one vs. big events). Curiosity makes informational interviews energizing — they genuinely want to learn about people's work. Kindness creates authentic connection. This leverages existing strengths rather than forcing a weakness-correction approach (enduring draining events). Option 1 ignores strengths. Option 3 abandons the goal. Option 4 tries to change their personality rather than working with it.
